did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses Deep Convolutional GANs (DCGANs) for synthetic image generation to address data scarcity and Convolutional Neural Networks (specifically GoogleNet/InceptionV3) for classifying skin lesions as benign or malignant in a dermatology context.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development of a skin cancer detection system using Deep Convolutional GANs (DCGANs) for synthetic image generation and Convolutional Neural Networks (specifically GoogleNet/InceptionV3) for classification. It details a specific methodology, dataset usage (HAM10000), implementation (Flutter/TFLite), and reports original performance results (accuracy, AUC, F1-score) from their experiments, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Skin Cancer Detection using GenAI
journal: 2025 7th International Conference on Information Systems and Computer Networks (ISCON)
brief_summary: This study addresses skin cancer detection by utilizing Deep Convolutional GANs (DCGANs) to generate synthetic dermoscopic images, thereby mitigating data scarcity and class imbalance in the HAM10000 dataset. The augmented data was used to train a GoogleNet (InceptionV3) Convolutional Neural Network for classifying lesions as benign or malignant. To ensure real-world applicability, the model was optimized using TensorFlow Lite and integrated into a Flutter-based Android application with a cloud-based API for real-time analysis. The system's performance was evaluated using metrics including AUC-ROC, F1-score, precision, and recall. The experimental results demonstrated a test accuracy of 77.23%, confirming the effectiveness of combining GAN-based augmentation with deep learning for robust skin cancer screening.

lesion_types_studied.details: Identified as one of the three main types of skin cancer widely known to the public. The study specifically addresses data scarcity for rare types like melanoma. The HAM10000 dataset used in the study contains images of melanoma. The model differentiates between melanoma and benign cases.
Listed as one of the three main types of skin cancer. The HAM10000 dataset used in the study contains images of BCC.
Listed as one of the three main types of skin cancer. The HAM10000 dataset used in the study contains images of SCC.

The classification task distinguishes between cancerous (malignant) and non-cancerous (benign) lesions. The HAM10000 dataset contains seven different types of skin lesions, including benign ones. The model classifies lesions as benign or malignant.

paper_terms: seven different types of skin lesions
lesion_types_studied.details: The study uses the HAM10000 dataset which comprises 7 different classes. The paper reports performance metrics (AUC, F1-score) for Class 0, Class 1, Class 2, Class 3, Class 4, Class 5, and Class 6, but does not explicitly name the specific lesion types for classes other than Melanoma, BCC, and SCC (which are implied to be among the 7 classes). Class 4 had the highest performance (Precision 0.8477, Recall 0.9402). Class 0 had the lowest scores.

all_task_types.target: Skin lesion categories (benign vs malignant, and specific classes from the HAM10000 dataset)
all_task_types.details: The paper describes a multiclass classification task using a GoogleNet (InceptionV3) model to classify skin lesions into seven different classes from the HAM10000 dataset. The model distinguishes between cancerous (malignant) and non-cancerous (benign) conditions, specifically identifying melanoma, basal cell carcinoma, squamous cell carcinoma, and other benign lesions. Performance was evaluated using accuracy (77.23%), AUC-ROC, precision, recall, and F1-score.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000
datasets_used.description: A publicly accessible, large, and diverse skin cancer dataset containing dermoscopy images of seven different types of skin lesions. It was used as the primary source for training and evaluating the proposed skin cancer detection system.

use.training: Used as the base dataset for generating synthetic images via DCGAN and for training the GoogleNet (InceptionV3) classification model.
use.testing: Used for evaluating the model's performance, including calculating accuracy (77.23%), AUC-ROC, F1-score, and generating confusion matrices.
use.other: Used to address data scarcity and class imbalance through GAN-based augmentation.

datasets_used.name: PH2
datasets_used.description: A dataset containing 200 high-quality dermoscopy images of different skin conditions, including normal moles and melanoma, often used for segmentation and classification tasks.

use.other: Mentioned in the literature review as an important dataset for training AI models for segmentation and classification tasks, but not explicitly used in the authors' experimental implementation.

datasets_used.name: ISIC (International Skin Imaging Collaboration)
datasets_used.description: A dataset created by the ISIC, containing over 13,000 images, used in many AI competitions to improve skin cancer diagnosis.

use.other: Mentioned in the literature review as a widely used dataset for training AI models in skin cancer detection, with some models achieving high accuracy (e.g., 93.48% on ISIC 2019).

statistical_methods_used.method: AUC-ROC (Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve)
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate the model's discriminative ability and balance between true positive and false positive rates across all seven classes of the HAM10000 dataset.
To visualize and quantify classification performance, including correct predictions and misclassifications for each of the seven lesion classes.

statistical_methods_used.method: Precision, Recall, and F1-score (Weighted and Macro Averages)
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To assess classification performance across classes, accounting for class imbalance (weighted average) and providing consistent performance metrics regardless of class frequency (macro average).
To measure the overall proportion of correctly classified instances in the test dataset.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics such as accuracy (77.23%), AUC-ROC, F1-score, precision, and recall, along with confusion matrices and comparison tables against other models.

HAM10000.notes: The model was trained for 25 epochs to classify all classes; 75 epochs resulted in over-training. Class 4 had the highest performance. Class 0 had the lowest scores due to limited support. The model effectively differentiated between melanoma and benign cases.

architecture_used.main_model: GoogleNet (InceptionV3) for classification; Deep Convolutional GAN (DCGAN) for data augmentation
architecture_used.pretraining: Not explicitly reported (described as 'fine-tuned' in context of augmentation, but no specific pretraining dataset like ImageNet is explicitly stated in the provided text snippets, though InceptionV3 is typically pretrained on ImageNet; however, sticking strictly to the text: 'fine-tuned GoogleNet (InceptionV3) model on the enriched dataset')

architecture_details.generator: Deep Convolutional GAN (DCGAN) using transposed convolutions, batch normalization, and nonlinear activation functions
architecture_details.discriminator: Binary classifier using convolutional layers with Leaky ReLU activations and dropout
architecture_details.classifier_backbone: GoogleNet (InceptionV3) with inception modules capturing hierarchical features at multiple scales via convolutional layers with varied filter sizes and batch normalization layers

training_details.preprocessing: Image standardization to 128x128x3; Normalization of pixel values to range [-1, 1]

preprocessing_techniques: Image Standardization: Resizing all input images to a uniform dimension of 128x128x3 pixels.
Normalization: Scaling pixel values to the range [-1, 1] to ensure consistency during model training.
Data Augmentation: Generating synthetic dermoscopic images using Deep Convolutional GANs (DCGANs) to address class imbalance and data scarcity.

data_augmentation_techniques.technique: Deep Convolutional GAN (DCGAN) Synthetic Image Generation
data_augmentation_techniques.details: Used to generate high-quality synthetic dermoscopic images to address data scarcity, dataset imbalance, and limited representation of diverse skin tones. The generator uses transposed convolutions, batch normalization, and nonlinear activation functions to transform random noise vectors into realistic images. The discriminator uses convolutional layers with Leaky ReLU activations and dropout to distinguish between real and synthetic images. This method was applied to the HAM10000 dataset to enrich the training data.

conclusions: The integration of Deep Convolutional GANs (DCGANs) for synthetic image generation effectively addresses data scarcity, class imbalance, and limited representation of diverse skin tones in the HAM10000 dataset.
The proposed GoogleNet (InceptionV3) model achieved a test accuracy of 77.23% and a weighted average F1-score of 0.7552, demonstrating robust performance in classifying seven types of skin lesions.
The model exhibited strong discriminative ability with AUC-ROC values ranging from 0.90 to 0.98 across all classes, with Class 6 achieving the highest AUC of 0.98.
Class 4 demonstrated the highest classification performance with 1,241 correct predictions, while Class 0 showed the lowest performance due to limited support in the dataset.

The GoogleNet model outperformed other tested architectures, including Custom Inception V3 (85.3%), RESNET 50 (88%), MobileNetV2 (78.79%), Normal CNN (65.27%), and DenseNet121 (90%), in the context of the specific implementation and deployment constraints.
Training the InceptionV3 model for 75 epochs resulted in over-training, yielding a single output, whereas 25 epochs successfully classified all classes.

The system was successfully optimized using TensorFlow Lite and integrated into a Flutter-based Android application with a cloud-based API, enabling real-time, non-invasive, and scalable skin cancer detection.

The study concludes that while deep learning improves early detection, real-world implementation requires better dataset diversity and model generalization to handle challenges like dataset bias and image quality issues.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the HAM10000 dataset for training and evaluation, which may introduce dataset bias and limit generalizability to other populations or imaging devices.
Class 0 exhibited extremely poor performance (F1-score: 0.0714, Recall: 0.0370) due to limited support in the dataset, indicating significant class imbalance issues despite GAN augmentation.

The reported test accuracy of 77.23% is notably lower than several baseline models cited in the comparison table (e.g., DenseNet121 at 90%, ResNet50 at 88%), raising questions about the efficacy of the proposed DCGAN+GoogleNet approach relative to standard architectures.
The paper does not report formal statistical significance testing (p-values) for the performance differences between models, relying solely on descriptive metrics.
The system architecture involves a cloud-based API for real-time analysis, which introduces potential data privacy and latency concerns for clinical deployment that are not addressed.
The model was trained for only 25 epochs; training for 75 epochs resulted in over-training (single output), suggesting potential under-training or sensitivity to hyperparameters.

Input images were resized to 128x128x3 pixels, which is significantly lower than the standard 224x224 resolution used by models like DenseNet121, potentially contributing to lower accuracy compared to other models.

The paper acknowledges that real-world implementation requires better dataset diversity and model generalization to handle challenges like dataset bias and image quality issues, highlighting a gap between experimental results and clinical applicability.
